Title: Biosynthesis and heterologous production of furanocoumarins: perspectives and current challenges

Common_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; review summarizes heterologous studies using diverse promoters in microbes/plants
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: General precursor-supply genes for engineering phenylpropanoid/coumarin/furanocoumarin production; useful for boosting aromatic amino acid pools and precursor conversion in microbial chassis, but should be tuned rather than maximized because excess tyrosine can reduce umbelliferone production.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases precursor supply from phenylalanine/tyrosine into coumarin/furanocoumarin biosynthesis, enabling umbelliferone formation and higher downstream product titers.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous pathway reconstruction with metabolite profiling in E. coli or yeast; overexpression/deletion engineering; titer comparison.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Carbon source feeding; precursor supplementation (tyrosine, p-coumaric acid, ferulic acid, caffeic acid); elicitation in plants by UVB, wounding, chemical elicitors, insects, fungal infection discussed generally.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not a single applied species; genes used broadly in reconstructed pathways.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: General phenylpropanoid pathway genes; tissue patterns not emphasized in this review.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq used in cited Angelica decursiva study to identify pathway genes; accession not provided in review.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Phenylalanine ammonia-lyase is the entry enzyme from phenylalanine to cinnamic acid; in many heterologous systems replaced functionally by TAL to bypass PAL+C4H.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Metabolic burden and precursor drain from primary metabolism in heterologous hosts.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PAL converts phenylalanine to cinnamic acid as the first committed phenylpropanoid step. 2. It supplies precursors for umbelliferone and downstream furanocoumarins. 3. In heterologous systems, TAL is often preferred as a shortcut from tyrosine to p-coumaric acid.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Plant tissues producing defense metabolites; specific organs vary by species
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Defense metabolites and therapeutics for skin disorders such as psoriasis and vitiligo

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful native step in plant pathways, but in microbial engineering often bypassed by TAL because CYP450-based C4H is harder to express in E. coli.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Converts cinnamic acid to p-coumaric acid, enabling phenylpropanoid flux to umbelliferone and furanocoumarins.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Known pathway biochemistry; functional expression attempts in heterologous hosts noted for AdC4H as unsuccessful in E. coli.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Precursor availability; eukaryotic expression system suitability affects interpretation.
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq used in cited Angelica decursiva study; accession not given.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: A cytochrome P450 monooxygenase; AdC4H did not functionally express in E. coli, supporting preference for eukaryotic hosts or TAL replacement.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Membrane-association and CPR dependence complicate heterologous expression.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. C4H converts cinnamic acid to p-coumaric acid in the native route. 2. As a membrane P450, it is difficult to express functionally in E. coli. 3. TAL often replaces PAL+C4H in microbial coumarin engineering.

Common_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: BL21; BW25113; MG1655; BY4742; WAT11/WAT21 cited for downstream CYPs
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Critical gateway enzyme to activate hydroxycinnamates to CoA esters; enzyme choice and protein engineering are highly impactful. Pc4CL1 and engineered Q272H/F267L variant are strong candidates for umbelliferone production.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Controls formation of p-coumaroyl-CoA/feruloyl-CoA/caffeoyl-CoA needed for coumarin and furanocoumarin precursor biosynthesis; often bottleneck in heterologous pathways.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Comparative heterologous expression of multiple orthologs; metabolite titer comparison; mutagenesis and optimization; in vivo production assays.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Culture/induction conditions; substrate feeding; tyrosine overproducing background strongly affects output.
Common_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: PAL/C4H or TAL upstream; C2'H/F6'H downstream
Common_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq in Angelica decursiva cited for Ad4CL discovery; accession not provided.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: At4CL1 higher catalytic efficiency toward p-coumaric acid; At4CL2 better for caffeic acid; Pc4CL2 broad specificity; Pc4CL1 gave highest umbelliferone titers and was improved by Q272H/F267L mutation to 356.59 mg/L.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Pathway bottleneck; poor enzyme choice can sharply limit titers; metabolic burden from overexpression.
Common_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petroselinum crispum; Oryza sativa; Angelica decursiva
Common_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Petroselinum crispum; Oryza sativa; Angelica decursiva
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 4CL activates hydroxycinnamic acids to CoA esters required for coumarin formation. 2. It is repeatedly identified as a heterologous bottleneck. 3. Ortholog selection and mutagenesis, especially Pc4CL1 Q272H/F267L, strongly improve umbelliferone titer.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Preferred shortcut enzyme for de novo microbial production because it bypasses PAL and difficult-to-express C4H. Strong candidate for metabolic engineering of umbelliferone/furanocoumarins.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Directly converts tyrosine to p-coumaric acid, increasing flux into umbelliferone and downstream furanocoumarin pathways.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: De novo pathway assembly in E. coli with metabolite quantification.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Tyrosine availability is a major determinant; excessive tyrosine may negatively affect umbelliferone production in some designs.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: RgTAL from Ruta graveolens and TAL from Saccharothrix espanaensis were used successfully for de novo umbelliferone production.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Flux imbalance from excessive precursor supply can reduce target yield; draws on aromatic amino acid pool.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TAL provides a direct tyrosine-to-p-coumaric acid shortcut. 2. It is widely used to simplify microbial coumarin/furanocoumarin pathways. 3. Its benefit depends on balanced tyrosine supply and downstream capacity.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Engineered microbial culture; in plants, precursor pathway broadly active
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Intermediate for defense and medicinal furanocoumarins

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; GST fusion used in E. coli to improve solubility in some studies
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Arabidopsis; Sweet potato; Rue; Peucedanum; Soybean; E. coli
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Ipomoea batatas; Ruta graveolens; Peucedanum praeruptorum; Glycine max; Escherichia coli
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Coumarin/furanocoumarin biosynthesis from hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA to umbelliferone/scopoletin
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core entry enzyme to form the coumarin scaffold; ortholog choice determines umbelliferone vs scopoletin bias. Strong engineering target for precursor module optimization.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates p-coumaroyl-CoA at the 2' position or feruloyl-CoA at the 6' position, enabling spontaneous trans-cis isomerization and lactonization to coumarins such as umbelliferone or scopoletin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Enables biosynthesis of umbelliferone, the direct precursor of furanocoumarins; substrate preference shapes coumarin product spectrum.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: cDNA screening/bioinformatics identification; recombinant enzyme assays; heterologous expression in E. coli; metabolite profiling.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Solubility limits in E. coli; GST fusion increases solubility/stability.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Identified in Arabidopsis thaliana, Ruta graveolens, Ipomoea batatas, Peucedanum praeruptorum, Glycine max, Angelica decursiva.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: AtF6'H1; AtF6'H2; RgC2'H; IbF6'H1-1; IbF6'H1-2; IbF6'H1-3; IbF6'H2-1-1; IbF6'H2-1-8; IbF6'H2-2-1; PpC2'H; GmF6'H; AdC2'H
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq cited for AdC2'H identification; accession not provided.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Substrate specificity varies by species: RgC2'H acts on both p-coumaroyl-CoA and feruloyl-CoA; PpC2'H strongly prefers p-coumaroyl-CoA; GmF6'H only uses feruloyl-CoA.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Poor solubility in bacteria; wrong ortholog may divert flux to scopoletin rather than umbelliferone.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 2'-hydroxylated or 6'-hydroxylated hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA intermediates leading to umbelliferone/scopoletin
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Important control point for coumarin formation; not explicitly named rate-limiting in review
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Arabidopsis thaliana; Ruta graveolens; Ipomoea batatas; Peucedanum praeruptorum; Glycine max; Angelica decursiva
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Arabidopsis thaliana; Ruta graveolens; Ipomoea batatas; Peucedanum praeruptorum; Glycine max; Angelica decursiva
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. C2'H/F6'H catalyzes the key ortho-hydroxylation step that creates the coumarin scaffold. 2. Orthologs differ strongly in preference for p-coumaroyl-CoA versus feruloyl-CoA. 3. Successful microbial expression enabled heterologous umbelliferone production.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Umbelliferone leading to psoralen, angelicin, xanthotoxin, bergapten and related furanocoumarins
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Furanocoumarin-accumulating plant tissues; engineered microbial cultures

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; transient Agrobacterium-mediated expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; N-terminal truncation advised for microbial expression
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Parsley; Parsnip; Fig; Citrus; Nicotiana benthamiana; Streptomyces xiamenensis; E. coli
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Petroselinum crispum; Pastinaca sativa; Ficus carica; Citrus limon; Nicotiana benthamiana; Streptomyces xiamenensis; Escherichia coli
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Furanocoumarin biosynthesis from umbelliferone to prenylated intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key gateway into linear and angular furanocoumarin biosynthesis; prime engineering target. N-terminal transit-peptide truncation may improve microbial performance.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a prenyl group from DMAPP to umbelliferone, mainly at C6 to form demethylsuberosin or at C8 to form osthenol depending on enzyme specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Determines entry into furanocoumarin biosynthesis and branch direction toward linear or angular products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro enzyme assays; transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; in vivo heterologous production in Streptomyces xiamenensis and E. coli.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor of target product
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: DMAPP supply can limit reaction; transit peptide removal affects heterologous function.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Transiently expressed in N. benthamiana; heterologously expressed in bacteria.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Uses DMAPP supplied by MVA/MEP pathway enzymes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PcPT prefers C6 prenylation; PsPT1 favors demethylsuberosin; PsPT2 favors osthenol; FcPT1a forms demethylsuberosin; ClPT1a instead uses GPP to form 8-geranylumbelliferone and is not considered furanocoumarin-specific.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Often poorly functional in yeast; plastid transit peptide may hinder microbial activity; limited DMAPP supply constrains production.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point and pathway entry step
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Petroselinum crispum; Pastinaca sativa; Ficus carica; Citrus limon
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Petroselinum crispum; Pastinaca sativa; Ficus carica; Citrus limon
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Plastid-associated membrane protein with N-terminal transit peptide
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PT catalyzes the first dedicated step into furanocoumarin biosynthesis. 2. Product regioselectivity determines linear versus angular branch entry. 3. Heterologous expression is challenging but possible in plant transient systems and some bacterial hosts.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Psoralen/angelicin and downstream hydroxylated or methoxylated furanocoumarins

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Ammi majus; Streptomyces xiamenensis; E. coli
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Ammi majus; Streptomyces xiamenensis; Escherichia coli
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Native plant gene remains unidentified, making this a major missing step. Promiscuous surrogate enzymes such as XimD can provisionally bridge pathway reconstruction.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes demethylsuberosin through CYP450-like oxidation to form marmesin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Generates the direct precursor of psoralen in the linear furanocoumarin branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical microsome assay in A. majus; surrogate heterologous production with XimD in S. xiamenensis and E. coli.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Requires NADPH and oxygen in microsomal assays; inhibited by cytochrome c and CO/blue light in biochemical study.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Detected biochemically in A. majus cells.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not identified for plant MS; XimD used as surrogate
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Likely CPR if native CYP450; XimD acts independently as surrogate monooxygenase
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Plant MS has not been molecularly characterized; only biochemical evidence exists. XimD from S. xiamenensis is a non-native workaround.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Major knowledge gap limits full pathway engineering; surrogate enzymes may have off-pathway promiscuity.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Native plant enzyme inferred CYP450; surrogate XimD is flavin-dependent monooxygenase
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Ammi majus; surrogate from Streptomyces xiamenensis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MS converts demethylsuberosin to marmesin, a necessary linear-branch intermediate. 2. The native plant gene is still unknown. 3. XimD can substitute this step in bacterial hosts as a temporary engineering solution.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Streptomyces xiamenensis; E. coli; plant enzyme not identified
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Native plant CS gene is unknown; currently a bottleneck for angular furanocoumarin engineering. Surrogate biocatalysts may be used temporarily.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cyclizes osthenol to columbianetin through oxidative rearrangement/hydroxylation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces the direct precursor of angelicin in the angular branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Surrogate heterologous production using XimD; no native plant molecular characterization.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: Not identified; XimD used as surrogate
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: No plant CS has been identified at biochemical or molecular level in the reviewed literature; XimD generated trace columbianetin.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Severe pathway uncertainty; surrogate enzyme low productivity/trace product in some hosts.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Unknown in plants; surrogate XimD is flavin-dependent monooxygenase
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Unknown plant source; surrogate from Streptomyces xiamenensis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CS should convert osthenol to columbianetin in the angular branch. 2. The native plant enzyme remains unidentified. 3. Surrogate XimD provides only limited proof-of-concept production.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; CYP73A1 N-terminal replacement improved expression in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Ammi majus; Apium graveolens; Pastinaca sativa; Psoralea corylifolia; Citrus paradisi; Heracleum lanatum; Peucedanum praeruptorum; S. cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Ammi majus; Apium graveolens; Pastinaca sativa; Psoralea corylifolia; Citrus paradisi; Heracleum lanatum; Peucedanum praeruptorum; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Variety: WAT11 yeast strain for many assays
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Essential enzyme to produce psoralen. Use CPR-expressing yeast and N-terminal engineering where needed. Valuable for pathway assembly toward medicinal linear furanocoumarins.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts marmesin to psoralen by oxidative ring closure/dehydration-like transformation mediated by CYP450 chemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces psoralen, the central linear furanocoumarin precursor for xanthotoxin, bergapten and related compounds.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification by homology; heterologous expression in S. cerevisiae WAT11; in vivo/in vitro enzyme assays; substrate specificity testing.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product or central intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Yeast expression improved by CYP73A1 N-terminal replacement and presence of AtCPR1.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Identified in elicited furanocoumarin-producing species; precise tissue profile not summarized.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CYP71AJ1; CYP71AJ2; CYP71AJ3; CYP71AJ49; putative P. corylifolia PS not functionally completed; possible C. paradisi PS; putative CYP71AJ27
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: AtCPR1; CYP73A1 anchor sequence used for engineering
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP71AJ1 from A. majus first molecularly characterized PS; CYP71AJ2/3 from celery/parsnip also function as PS; P. praeruptorum CYP71AJ49 characterized; some homologs remain putative only.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Membrane-association and codon/anchor issues hinder expression in microbes.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Ammi majus; Apium graveolens; Pastinaca sativa; Peucedanum praeruptorum and others
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Ammi majus; Apium graveolens; Pastinaca sativa; Peucedanum praeruptorum
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PS converts marmesin to psoralen with high substrate specificity. 2. Several CYP71AJ members from Apiaceae are functional PSs. 3. Yeast expression often needs CPR support and sometimes N-terminal engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; CYP73A1 N-terminal replacement used for P. praeruptorum AS in yeast
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Pastinaca sativa; Peucedanum praeruptorum; S. cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Pastinaca sativa; Peucedanum praeruptorum; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Required for engineering angelicin and angular furanocoumarins; should be paired with angular-branch PT/CS activities and suitable CYP expression platform.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts columbianetin to angelicin via CYP450-catalyzed oxidative ring closure.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces angelicin, the key angular furanocoumarin scaffold.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional expression in S. cerevisiae WAT11; in vivo and in vitro assays; homology-guided gene identification.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms target product or central intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Yeast expression enhanced by N-terminal engineering and CPR support.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: AtCPR1; CYP73A1 anchor sequence for engineered expression
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CYP71AJ4 from parsnip and CYP71AJ51 from P. praeruptorum are validated AS enzymes; CYP71AJ3 and AJ4 share ~70% identity but differ in substrate recognition sites.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: CYP expression challenges similar to PS; angular branch still constrained by unknown upstream CS gene.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AS converts columbianetin to angelicin in the angular branch. 2. Validated AS enzymes belong to the CYP71AJ family. 3. Heterologous expression works in CPR-equipped yeast with N-terminal optimization when needed.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Necessary to access xanthotoxol/xanthotoxin branch from psoralen; useful for assembling methoxylated linear furanocoumarin pathways.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates psoralen at position 8 to form xanthotoxol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Channels psoralen into the xanthotoxin branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Molecular characterization and in vitro microsome assays in yeast expressing CYP71AZ4.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Requires functional CYP system in yeast; accepts several simple coumarins besides psoralen.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Shows substrate preference for psoralen but can also accept some simple coumarins such as scopoletin.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Possible substrate promiscuity may create by-products in engineered systems.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. P8H converts psoralen to xanthotoxol. 2. CYP71AZ4 from parsnip is the first molecularly characterized P8H. 3. The enzyme is suitable for yeast-based reconstruction of the xanthotoxin pathway.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Biochemically validated but native gene remains unidentified; a missing step for complete bergapten pathway engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates psoralen at position 5 to form bergaptol.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies bergaptol precursor for bergapten biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical characterization in A. majus cell suspensions/microsomes.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not detailed in review beyond biochemical assays.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Biochemical activity in A. majus cells.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unknown gene limits pathway transfer and optimization.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. P5H converts psoralen to bergaptol. 2. Only biochemical evidence exists in A. majus. 3. Lack of gene identification remains a bottleneck for bergapten biosynthesis engineering.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Ammi majus; Glehnia littoralis; Angelica dahurica; Peucedanum praeruptorum; E. coli
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Ammi majus; Glehnia littoralis; Angelica dahurica; Peucedanum praeruptorum; Escherichia coli
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A validated, portable SAM-dependent methylation step for bergapten biosynthesis. Suitable for microbial production because endogenous SAM can support in vivo activity, but SAM boosting may further help.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Transfers a methyl group from SAM to bergaptol to form bergapten.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces bergapten from bergaptol with high substrate specificity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Recombinant expression in E. coli; in vitro substrate specificity assays; in vivo conversion assays to bergapten.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Depends on SAM supply; endogenous E. coli SAM can suffice in vivo.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Heterologous expression in E. coli successful.
Pathway_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: AdB5OMT; PpB5OMT and additional B5OMTs from A. majus and G. littoralis (specific IDs not provided)
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Uses SAM generated by MetK/SAM pathway
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Distinct from xanthotoxol OMT because it does not accept xanthotoxol; E. coli secretes produced bergapten to medium. AdB5OMT showed higher in vivo than in vitro activity, whereas PpB5OMT behaved similarly in both.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: SAM limitation may cap titers; methyltransferase specificity excludes xanthotoxin branch.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Ammi majus; Glehnia littoralis; Angelica dahurica; Peucedanum praeruptorum
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Ammi majus; Glehnia littoralis; Angelica dahurica; Peucedanum praeruptorum
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. B5OMT specifically methylates bergaptol to bergapten. 2. Multiple plant orthologs are functionally expressed in E. coli. 3. Endogenous microbial SAM is often sufficient, but SAM-pathway engineering could further improve production.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential enzyme for xanthotoxin branch completion, but still unvalidated; should be treated as tentative until biochemical proof is obtained.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Putatively methylates xanthotoxol at position 8 to form xanthotoxin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Likely contributes to xanthotoxin production, but function remains unconfirmed in review.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Candidate identification only; no full functional validation reported.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Would directly form target product if validated
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Identified as a putative candidate in G. littoralis.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The review explicitly notes lack of molecular/biochemical characterization despite recent identification of a putative candidate.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Uncertain function makes pathway assembly risky.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. X8OMT is proposed to methylate xanthotoxol to xanthotoxin. 2. A candidate was recently identified in G. littoralis. 3. Definitive functional validation is still missing.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; expressed efficiently in S. cerevisiae WAT11/WAT21 with AtCPR1/2 backgrounds
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Citrus hystrix; Citrus paradisi; Ammi majus; Pastinaca sativa; S. cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Citrus hystrix; Citrus paradisi; Ammi majus; Pastinaca sativa; Saccharomyces cerevisiae
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Late linear furanocoumarin branch toward isopimpinellin
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for building late hydroxylated xanthotoxin derivatives; requires robust CYP redox support in yeast.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates xanthotoxin at position 5 to form 5-hydroxy-xanthotoxin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Produces a direct precursor to isopimpinellin-like late products.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Molecular identification and functional expression in yeast; enzyme assays showing activity toward xanthotoxin but not bergapten.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Expression benefited from AtCPR1 or AtCPR2 overexpressing yeast strains.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Validated in both Apiaceae and Citrus; does not hydroxylate bergapten.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: CYP dependency complicates bacterial deployment; substrate selectivity excludes bergapten route.
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species: Ammi majus; Pastinaca sativa; Citrus hystrix; Citrus paradisi
Pathway_Genes.Source_Species_Latin_Name: Ammi majus; Pastinaca sativa; Citrus hystrix; Citrus paradisi
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. X5H converts xanthotoxin to 5-hydroxy-xanthotoxin. 2. Enzymes from Apiaceae and Citrus were functionally verified in CPR-enhanced yeast strains. 3. They are specific for xanthotoxin and inactive toward bergapten.

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Species: Not identified in any source species
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Missing gene step; currently a major obstacle to reconstruct complete bergapten-derived late pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Would hydroxylate bergapten at position 8 to form 8-hydroxy-bergapten.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Expected precursor-forming step toward isopimpinellin from bergapten branch.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: No biochemical or molecular validation reported.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Unknown enzyme prevents full pathway completion.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. B8H is postulated to convert bergapten to 8-hydroxy-bergapten. 2. No molecular or biochemical characterization is available. 3. This missing step contributes to uncertainty in late furanocoumarin biosynthesis.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Missing terminal methyltransferase; unresolved gene limits complete isopimpinellin pathway engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Predicted final methylation step toward isopimpinellin from the xanthotoxin branch.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Would depend on SAM, but not experimentally shown.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 5HX5OMT is a proposed final methylation step to isopimpinellin. 2. No enzyme has been characterized. 3. The terminal branch remains speculative.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Alternative missing terminal methyltransferase route to isopimpinellin; unresolved and currently unusable for rational engineering.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Predicted terminal conversion from bergapten branch to isopimpinellin.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned only as a hypothetical enzyme; pathway unresolved.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No identified gene or activity data.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. 8HX8OMT is proposed as an alternative terminal route to isopimpinellin. 2. No biochemical or molecular evidence is available. 3. The late bergapten-derived route remains uncertain.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Knockout of TyrR relieves aromatic amino acid pathway repression and is broadly useful to increase tyrosine supply for de novo umbelliferone/furanocoumarin engineering, but should be balanced to avoid excess tyrosine effects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Relieves repression of aromatic amino acid biosynthesis, generally improving tyrosine availability and supporting higher coumarin precursor production.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene deletion in engineered E. coli strains combined with product titer comparison.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major nutrient-responsive transcriptional repressor controlling precursor availability rather than the dedicated furanocoumarin pathway itself.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Effect depends on carbon source and pathway configuration; too much tyrosine can negatively affect umbelliferone production.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Not applicable beyond endogenous E. coli.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedback/homeostatic regulator responsive to aromatic amino acid end products, especially tyrosine.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Regulates aroG; tyrA and other aromatic pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Precursor supply for aromatic amino acid biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review states native enzymes are feedback inhibited by tyrosine/phenylalanine and by tyrosine repressor TyrR.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can disturb amino acid homeostasis and create flux imbalance or growth burden when combined with multiple precursor-boosting manipulations.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: aroG; tyrA and broader aromatic amino acid biosynthetic regulon
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses aromatic amino acid biosynthesis genes, limiting tyrosine accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. TyrR constrains aromatic precursor supply by repressing tyrosine-pathway genes. 2. Deleting tyrR is a common strategy to increase tyrosine availability for coumarin production. 3. Benefits depend on maintaining balanced precursor flux.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Tyrosine; phenylalanine; aromatic amino acid status

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Deletion of ybgC protects hydroxycinnamoyl-CoA intermediates from hydrolysis and is a useful chassis-level edit to stabilize pathway intermediates.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduces degradation of p-coumaroyl-CoA and caffeoyl-CoA, significantly improving coumarin pathway output.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene deletion with metabolite titer improvement in engineered E. coli.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts as a negative controller of pathway intermediate stability; removing it directly preserves metabolic flux to coumarins.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Most evident under heterologous production conditions accumulating CoA esters.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Though not a classic TF, it is a decisive endogenous negative regulator of coumarin production because it hydrolyzes relevant thioesters.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Deletion may affect native thioester metabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Does not regulate genes directly; depletes key CoA-esters and thereby reduces pathway flux
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. YbgC hydrolyzes pathway CoA esters. 2. Deleting ybgC significantly improves coumarin production in engineered E. coli. 3. This edit helps preserve activated intermediates for downstream conversion.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Intermediate for defense and medicinal compounds

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Saccharomyces cerevisiae; Escherichia coli and others
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Overexpression of CPR is one of the most effective enabling strategies for plant CYP450 function in heterologous hosts; should be co-optimized with CYP level and N-terminal engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Improves activity of plant CYP450 enzymes such as PS, AS, P8H and X5H, thereby increasing downstream furanocoumarin formation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Co-expression in engineered yeast strains WAT11/WAT21 and other systems; functional CYP assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential redox partner enabling multiple decisive oxidative steps in the pathway.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Host membrane environment and CYP N-terminal architecture condition CPR effectiveness.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Native reductase in Arabidopsis thaliana; not detailed.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: CYP71AJ1; CYP71AJ2; CYP71AJ3; CYP71AJ4; CYP71AJ49; CYP71AJ51; CYP71AZ4; CYP71AZ1; CYP71AZ6; CYP82D64v1; CYP82D64v2
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: WAT11 and WAT21 strains overexpress AtCPR1 or AtCPR2 to enhance plant CYP activity.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Imbalanced CYP:CPR ratios may reduce efficiency; membrane burden in host cells.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Multiple plant CYP450 enzymes in the furanocoumarin pathway
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Forms functional electron-transfer partnership with membrane-bound CYP450s
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Enhances catalytic activity of target CYP enzymes rather than gene transcription
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPR is required to support plant CYP450 activity in heterologous hosts. 2. Overexpressing AtCPR1 or AtCPR2 improves function of several furanocoumarin-pathway CYPs. 3. CPR co-expression is a core design rule for pathway reconstruction.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Multiple furanocoumarins downstream of CYP steps
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Engineered microbial cultures and plant tissues

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Key rate-limiting enzyme for increasing DMAPP supply in the mevalonate pathway; truncated versions are especially useful in yeast to elevate prenyl donor availability for PT reactions.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases mevalonate-pathway flux and DMAPP availability, supporting prenyltransferase-dependent entry into furanocoumarin biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Pathway engineering studies summarized in review; overexpression/truncation associated with improved prenyl donor supply.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major bottleneck controller of prenyl donor production for the dedicated PT step.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Depends on host pathway context and feedback regulation; truncated tHMGR removes inhibitory N-terminus.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Subject to feedback regulation in native state; truncation removes this control.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: MVA pathway enzymes; PT indirectly via DMAPP provision
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: DMAPP/prenyl donor precursor supply via mevalonate pathway
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review identifies HMGR as rate-limiting and specifically recommends truncated HMGR lacking 530 N-terminal amino acids.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excess flux to isoprenoids can burden cells and divert acetyl-CoA from growth.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: MVA-pathway flux to mevalonate and DMAPP
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Flux control by catalytic bottleneck and feedback relief
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Does not directly regulate genes; increases prenyl donor availability for PT-mediated product formation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HMGR is a major control point in the MVA pathway for DMAPP supply. 2. Truncated HMGR is commonly used to relieve feedback regulation. 3. Improving HMGR activity can enhance prenyltransferase-dependent furanocoumarin biosynthesis.
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Acetyl-CoA availability; native feedback by pathway state

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; stronger promoters often used in engineering
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Core MEP-pathway control point to raise DMAPP supply for prenyltransferase reactions in bacterial chassis; strong target for promoter replacement or overexpression.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increases MEP-pathway flux toward IPP/DMAPP, improving prenyl donor availability for PT-dependent furanocoumarin entry step.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: General metabolic engineering evidence summarized in review.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Rate-limiting gateway that strongly constrains DMAPP formation in bacterial hosts.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Subject to feedback inhibition by DMAPP and IPP.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedback inhibited by DMAPP and IPP.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: DMAPP precursor supply via MEP pathway
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review explicitly names DXS as a rate-limiting enzyme of the MEP pathway.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can increase metabolic burden and imbalance upstream carbon allocation.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Flux control by catalytic bottleneck and feedback sensitivity
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: No direct gene regulation; enhances DMAPP pool that drives PT step
Regulation_Genes.Source_Species: Bacteria; heterologous versions from Bacillus subtilis, Rhodobacter capsulatus, Paracoccus aminophilus and others discussed
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXS is a principal rate-limiting step in the MEP pathway. 2. Overexpression or promoter strengthening can raise DMAPP supply. 3. This is valuable for improving furanocoumarin prenylation in bacterial hosts.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Not specified; stronger promoters often used in engineering
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Another key MEP-pathway bottleneck to improve DMAPP supply for prenylation; commonly co-overexpressed with DXS.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Boosts MEP-pathway throughput and DMAPP availability, supporting prenyltransferase-dependent furanocoumarin formation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: General metabolic engineering evidence summarized in review.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Low-activity control point that can limit prenyl donor formation in bacteria.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: DMAPP precursor supply via MEP pathway
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review identifies DXR as rate-limiting due to low activity.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Extra enzyme expression may impose burden without balanced downstream modules.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Flux control by low catalytic capacity
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: No direct transcriptional regulation; boosts prenyl-donor biosynthesis
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DXR is another rate-limiting step of the MEP pathway. 2. Its overexpression is used to raise DMAPP supply. 3. Improving DXR helps support PT-driven entry into furanocoumarin biosynthesis.

Regulation_Genes.Applied_Species_Latin_Name: Escherichia coli; Saccharomyces cerevisiae and others
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for increasing DMAPP specifically from IPP in hosts where prenyl donor balance is limiting; can improve PT substrate supply.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Shifts IPP/DMAPP equilibrium toward DMAPP, benefiting prenyltransferase reactions.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Metabolic engineering strategies summarized in review.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important donor-balance controller where DMAPP is specifically limiting for PT activity.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Balances IPP and DMAPP pools homeostatically.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Explicitly recommended in review as a strategy to increase DMAPP in yeast.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May not help if total isoprenoid flux rather than pool ratio is limiting.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: No direct gene regulation; raises DMAPP available for PT
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IDI adjusts the balance between IPP and DMAPP. 2. Overexpression can increase DMAPP availability. 3. This can improve prenylation efficiency in heterologous furanocoumarin pathways.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Overexpression of metK or yeast SAM2 is a direct strategy to support O-methyltransferase steps such as B5OMT and likely other unresolved methylation steps.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Raises SAM availability, supporting methyltransferase-dependent formation of bergapten, xanthotoxin-like and isopimpinellin-like products.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: General pathway engineering rationale summarized in review; in vivo OMT activity observed with endogenous SAM, but higher production often requires pathway optimization.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Controls methyl donor supply for multiple terminal OMT steps.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Methionine supplementation and host SAM-pathway state influence effect.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Engineered overexpression in bacteria or yeast.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: SAM pathway is subject to feedback/homeostatic regulation via MetJ/MetR/metA/cysE network; SAM2 less feedback-sensitive than SAM1 in yeast.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: B5OMT and other OMTs via SAM provision
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review emphasizes metK overexpression in bacteria and SAM2 over SAM1 in yeast because SAM2 is not inhibited by SAM accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can perturb methionine/SAM homeostasis and impose ATP cost.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: No direct gene regulation; enhances OMT-catalyzed target-product formation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. SAM synthase controls SAM availability for methylation steps. 2. Overexpressing metK or yeast SAM2 can support OMT-dependent furanocoumarin biosynthesis. 3. Cofactor supply becomes increasingly important as late methylated products are targeted.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Deleting metJ derepresses methionine/SAM biosynthetic genes and can increase methyl donor supply for OMT steps in furanocoumarin engineering.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Relieves repression of methionine biosynthesis, increasing SAM-pathway flux and potentially boosting methylated furanocoumarin production.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: General engineering strategy described in review.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Acts as a central repressor of SAM precursor biosynthesis, thus influencing all SAM-dependent OMT steps.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Responsive to methionine/SAM status in cell.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Part of homeostatic methionine/SAM feedback network.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: MetR; methionine biosynthetic genes including metA/metH/metE network
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes MetJ inhibits expression of several methionine biosynthetic genes; therefore deletion is often used.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Disrupts sulfur amino acid homeostasis and may affect growth.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Represses methionine biosynthetic genes, lowering SAM precursor supply
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MetJ represses methionine biosynthetic genes. 2. Its deletion is used to increase SAM-pathway flux. 3. This can benefit OMT-dependent furanocoumarin methylation steps.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Overexpressing MetR can enhance methionine synthase expression and support SAM supply for methyltransferase-dependent late furanocoumarin steps.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Activates methionine synthase expression, promoting methionine/SAM formation and supporting OMT reactions.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: General engineering strategy summarized in review.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Positive regulator of methionine/SAM biosynthesis feeding methylation-dependent product formation.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: MetH; MetE and methionine pathway genes
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review specifically notes MetR activates transcription of methionine synthase genes MetH/MetE.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: May perturb sulfur metabolism if overexpressed excessively.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates methionine synthase genes to increase methionine and downstream SAM availability
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MetR positively regulates methionine synthase genes. 2. Overexpressing MetR can enhance SAM precursor formation. 3. This strategy may improve OMT-dependent furanocoumarin biosynthesis.
